timeline.detail: CPD begins using the Strategic Subject List. Algorithm uses prior arrests, gun-related charges, gang affiliation, age, and associates' arrest history. No independent validation before deployment. No named CPD official documented as accountable for list accuracy or equity.

timeline.event: Custom notification visits — police visit listed individuals before any offence
timeline.detail: CPD uses SSL scores to direct officers to visit high-scoring individuals at home. Individuals are not told they are on the list. Visits are experienced as intimidating by recipients and families. List grows to ~400,000 by 2016.

timeline.event: RAND audit — no evidence of effectiveness
timeline.detail: RAND Corporation publishes an independent audit finding no statistically significant evidence that the SSL reduced gun violence or changed the behaviour of listed individuals. CPD continues using the SSL despite RAND findings.

timeline.event: Chicago Inspector General — racial disparity documented; CPD cannot explain methodology
timeline.detail: The Chicago OIG investigates the SSL and finds the list disproportionately contains Black men from specific neighbourhoods. OIG also finds CPD cannot explain the algorithm's methodology in sufficient detail to assess its equity implications — a C3 and C5 failure confirmed by the oversight body.

timeline.event: SSL terminated — RAND findings cited
timeline.detail: CPD announces termination of the Strategic Subject List. No individual is held accountable for seven years of pre-crime scoring of 400,000 people with no evidence of effectiveness.

conditions.name: Named Accountable Owner
text: No named CPD official was documented as accountable for the accuracy or equity of the SSL throughout seven years of deployment. The algorithm was developed externally and operationalised without a named official whose role required certifying validity before it directed police activity toward 400,000 individuals.

CPD commissioning RAND to evaluate whether their algorithm worked is itself evidence of C1 absence: a named accountable owner would have had the documented responsibility to produce that evaluation internally and continuously, not as a one-time external commission three years in.
conditions.quote: CPD commissioned RAND to find out if their algorithm worked. No named official had been accountable for answering that question internally.

conditions.name: Independent Validation
text: The SSL was not independently validated for accuracy or equity before deployment. The fundamental validation question — does a high score predict involvement in gun violence at a rate justifying pre-emptive police attention? — was unanswered before use. RAND's independent evaluation, conducted three years after deployment, found no evidence it did.

Use of gang affiliation and associates' arrest records as inputs creates a specific validation requirement: testing against populations with and without these characteristics to determine whether they add predictive validity beyond the demographic proxies they represent. That test was never conducted.

conditions.name: Structured Monitoring
text: No structured monitoring tracked whether listed individuals who received custom notification visits were subsequently involved in gun violence at lower rates than comparable individuals not on the list — the basic evaluation of whether the programme was working. The OIG investigation found CPD could not explain the algorithm's methodology to an oversight body with formal authority — monitoring gap and accountability gap are the same gap.

conditions.name: Pre-Established Accountability Protocol
text: No pre-established protocol gave listed individuals any right to know their score, challenge the data generating it, or seek removal. No protocol required CPD to suspend visits if the algorithm's outputs could not be independently validated. The programme continued for four years after RAND found no evidence of effectiveness because there was no governance obligation requiring suspension.

conditions.name: No Active Suppression
text: Suppression is the non-disclosure of list membership to 400,000 scored individuals. People were visited by police and had their liberty and reputation affected by their SSL status with no way of knowing they were on the list, what their score was, or what generated it. CPD policy was explicitly not to tell individuals their SSL status.

The OIG's finding that CPD could not explain the algorithm's methodology to a body with formal oversight authority is the institutional suppression confirmation: a programme that operated for seven years without its methodology being disclosable to oversight.

laundering.desc: L-13: Violence-prevention framing used to deflect scrutiny of equity implications and absent evidence of effectiveness. L-07: Data-driven policing framed as objective — removing human bias — when the algorithm encoded the same biases as its historical training data. L-16: Individuals bore consequences of their score without any mechanism to challenge it.

prevention.title: Named CPD official accountable for SSL accuracy and equity from deployment
prevention.desc: One named official with an annual equity report — their name on it — would have required the RAND evaluation's questions to be answered internally before any external commission. Their documented responsibility to monitor means the absence of effectiveness evidence would have required action within the first year, not the seventh.

prevention.title: Independent validation of predictive accuracy against a held-out sample before deployment
prevention.desc: Testing the SSL on individuals whose subsequent outcomes were known — before the algorithm was used — would have revealed the absence of predictive validity RAND later documented. Validation before deployment is not an added cost; it is the minimum evidence that a system has the effect it claims.

prevention.title: Quarterly tracking of post-visit outcomes compared to control population
prevention.desc: For every individual receiving a custom notification visit based on their SSL score: were they subsequently involved in gun violence? At what rate compared to comparable individuals not visited? The data existed. The requirement to produce and review it did not.

prevention.title: A right to know, challenge, and seek removal from the SSL
prevention.desc: Any individual whose SSL score directed police activity toward them should have had a right to know their score, what data generated it, and a formal challenge mechanism. Credit reporting dispute rights are an analogue. Their absence meant data errors compounded across seven years with no correction pathway.

prevention.title: Mandatory OIG disclosure of full methodology for any algorithmic policing tool
prevention.desc: A requirement that CPD produce and maintain a full methodology document for any algorithmic risk-scoring tool — disclosable to the OIG on request before deployment — would have made the SSL's methodology examinable before seven years of secret operation.

comparables.name: PredPol / Geolitica
comparables.desc: Direct comparator: predictive policing deployed without independent validation, terminated after audit confirmed bias amplification and no effectiveness evidence. PredPol predicts locations; SSL predicts individuals. Together they define the two scales of predictive policing failure.

comparables.name: CalGang Database
comparables.desc: Domain comparator: law enforcement risk database, subjects not notified, no meaningful challenge mechanism, racial disparity confirmed by state audit. SSL scored individuals by risk; CalGang labelled individuals by gang affiliation. Together they define the two law enforcement database governance failures.

comparables.name: COMPAS Risk Assessment
comparables.desc: Structural comparator: algorithmic risk scoring in criminal justice, documented racial disparity, no independent validation before deployment, subjects cannot challenge classification. COMPAS in sentencing, SSL in pre-crime policing: same governance failures at adjacent pipeline points.

metadata.Suppression Confirmed: Yes — list membership not disclosed to scored individuals; methodology not disclosable to OIG
metadata.Harm Scale: 400,000 individuals scored · no evidence of effectiveness · terminated after RAND audit

los-angeles-county-raps-gang-database-ca.overviewTitle: 42 people listed as gang members before their first birthday. 23 listed with no documented reason.
los-angeles-county-raps-gang-database-ca.overviewNote: CalGang was California's statewide gang database used by law enforcement agencies across the state. A 2016 state audit found 42 individuals entered before age one, 23 with no documented basis, and a removal process so inaccessible that individuals remained listed for years after any valid basis lapsed. Entries shaped immigration enforcement, housing, employment, and criminal prosecution for two decades.
overview: CalGang was California's statewide law enforcement gang database. Police departments entered individuals based on self-admission, wearing gang colours, or associating with known gang members. Entries were shared across jurisdictions and used by federal immigration authorities, housing agencies, and prosecutors. By 2016, the database contained approximately 150,000 entries.

The 2016 California State Auditor report found fundamental governance failures: 42 individuals entered before age one; 23 with no documented reason; entries frequently failing the database's own criteria; and a dispute and removal process effectively inaccessible. Database entries directly affected immigration proceedings, employment background checks, and housing applications. California mandated reforms via AB 90 in 2020 and significantly restricted the database's use. Multiple jurisdictions discontinued participation. No remedy was established for individuals harmed by incorrect entries over two decades.

timeline.event: CalGang deployed statewide — no independent audit mechanism
timeline.detail: California's statewide gang database goes live. Entry criteria include self-admission, wearing colours, and associating with gang members. No independent audit of entry accuracy established. No meaningful removal mechanism exists. Federal immigration authorities gain access.

timeline.event: Database shapes immigration, housing, employment — subjects cannot challenge
timeline.detail: CalGang entries are used by ICE in immigration proceedings, housing authorities in tenancy decisions, and employers in background checks. Individuals have no reliable mechanism to learn of inclusion, challenge its basis, or seek removal when criteria no longer apply. Entries persist for years beyond any documented basis.

timeline.event: California State Auditor report — systemic failures confirmed
timeline.detail: The California State Auditor publishes a comprehensive audit finding: 42 individuals listed before age one; 23 with no documented reason; widespread non-compliance with entry criteria; removal process effectively inaccessible; insufficient oversight of local agency use. Nearly 20 years of operation without meaningful accuracy review.

timeline.event: Los Angeles County restricts CalGang access
timeline.detail: Following the audit, LA County significantly restricts who can access CalGang data and for what purposes. The ACLU and community groups file challenges to the database's use in immigration and criminal proceedings.

timeline.event: AB 90 — mandatory reforms and restricted use
timeline.detail: California passes AB 90 imposing new entry standards, requiring regular audits, creating a clearer removal process, and restricting circumstances for database use. Multiple jurisdictions discontinue participation. Reforms are prospective — no remedy for individuals harmed over two decades.

conditions.name: Named Accountable Owner
text: No named state official was documented as accountable for CalGang entry accuracy across hundreds of contributing agencies. Each agency entered data under its own standards with no named state official holding documented responsibility for verifying entries met the database's own criteria.

The auditor's finding — 42 individuals listed before age one — is the operational consequence of C1 absence in a distributed system: when no one is named as accountable for accuracy across contributing agencies, no one checks.
conditions.quote: 42 people were listed as gang members before their first birthday. The system had no named owner for accuracy.

conditions.name: Independent Validation
text: CalGang's entry criteria — self-admission, wearing colours, association — were never independently validated as accurate predictors of gang membership. These criteria are subjective and officer-discretion dependent. No study assessed whether individuals entered on these criteria were actually gang members at rates justifying the database's downstream consequences.

The auditor finding 23 individuals with no documented reason confirms that even the database's own criteria — however weak — were not consistently applied. A database whose criteria are not consistently applied has not been validated on any standard.

conditions.name: Structured Monitoring
text: No structured monitoring tracked CalGang entry accuracy against verifiable outcomes. The state auditor's 2016 findings were the first systematic external review in nearly 20 years of operation. Annual monitoring of a random sample would have identified the age-one entries and no-documented-reason entries within the first two years.

conditions.name: Pre-Established Accountability Protocol
text: No pre-established protocol gave listed individuals meaningful rights to challenge entry or seek removal when basis lapsed. Automatic temporal limits existed on paper but no mechanism ensured expired entries were removed. The challenge process was documented as effectively inaccessible by the state auditor — inaccessibility was the system's default after 20 years of operation.

conditions.name: No Active Suppression
text: Suppression is the non-disclosure of database membership to listed individuals. People suffered consequences — immigration enforcement, housing denial, employment impact — from a database listing they did not know existed and had no mechanism to discover. The database was accessible to law enforcement and federal immigration authorities but not to the individuals it described.

The auditor's finding that the removal process was effectively inaccessible is the suppression confirmation: even individuals who knew they might be listed could not meaningfully challenge their entry. Inaccessibility was not incidental — it was the system's operational default after two decades without a functioning accountability mechanism.

laundering.desc: L-04: Each year's continued operation justified by previous year's established practice, deferring the accuracy question indefinitely. L-08: Individual harms from immigration proceedings and housing denials were not aggregated into a database-level accuracy assessment. L-16: Individuals bore consequences of inaccurate entries without any practical challenge mechanism.

prevention.title: Named state official accountable for CalGang data quality across all contributing agencies
prevention.desc: One named state official with documented accountability for the accuracy of all entries — not just technical operation — would have required an annual accuracy audit. Their name on that audit would have identified age-one entries and no-documented-reason entries within the first year of operation.

prevention.title: Independent validation of entry criteria against confirmed gang membership
prevention.desc: CalGang's criteria should have been independently tested — evaluated against a sample of entered individuals to assess whether each criterion produced confirmed gang members at rates justifying the database's downstream consequences. The auditor's findings suggest this was never done.

prevention.title: Annual independent audit of a random sample of database entries
prevention.desc: An annual independent audit of 500 randomly selected entries — 0.3% of the database — assessing: documented criterion, criterion meeting database's own standards, criterion not expired. This monitoring would have identified the systematic accuracy failures within the first two years of operation.

prevention.title: Written notification to listed individuals and accessible independent removal process
prevention.desc: Every individual entered into CalGang should have received written notification within 30 days: their listing, its basis, and how to challenge it. Removal requests decided by an independent body, not the entering agency. Automatic removal at entry expiry built into the system. These are minimum due process requirements for a database affecting liberty.

prevention.title: Statutory right for individuals to access their own CalGang record
prevention.desc: A right — enforceable through the courts — for any individual to request and receive their CalGang record, entry basis, and name of the entering officer would have created the accountability mechanism the audit found absent. Disclosure would have also enabled individuals to identify the kind of errors — listed before age one, no documented reason — the auditor found at scale.

comparables.name: Chicago Strategic Subject List
comparables.desc: Closest structural comparator: law enforcement risk database, subjects not notified, no meaningful challenge mechanism, racial disparity, terminated after audit. SSL scored individuals; CalGang labelled them. Together they define the two dominant law enforcement database governance failure patterns.

comparables.name: COMPAS Risk Assessment
comparables.desc: Structural comparator: algorithmic classification in criminal justice, racial disparity in outputs, no challenge mechanism for subjects. COMPAS in sentencing, CalGang in database listing: same governance failure at different pipeline points.

comparables.name: Netherlands Toeslagenaffaire
comparables.desc: Cross-domain comparator: government database using discriminatory proxy criteria, no accessible challenge mechanism, confirmed suppression. Toeslagenaffaire used dual nationality in fraud scoring; CalGang used association and appearance in gang labelling. Same mechanism, different domain.

metadata.Suppression Confirmed: Yes — database membership not disclosed to listed individuals
metadata.Harm Scale: 150,000 entries · 42 listed before age 1 · 23 no documented reason · immigration/housing/employment impact

dna-probabilistic-genotyping-software-tr.overviewTitle: Criminal defendants were convicted on software evidence they were legally prevented from examining.
dna-probabilistic-genotyping-software-tr.overviewNote: TrueAllele and STRmix calculate likelihood ratios from mixed DNA samples and are used as evidence in criminal prosecutions. Vendors refuse source code disclosure even under court order. Courts are split on whether defendants have a constitutional right to examine the software that produced evidence used to convict them. Bugs found after convictions.
overview: Probabilistic genotyping software analyses mixed DNA samples and produces statistical likelihood ratios used as forensic evidence in criminal trials. Two systems dominate US and international use: TrueAllele (Cybergenetics) and STRmix (ESR New Zealand and the FBI). These likelihood ratios are presented as objective scientific evidence, with the statistical precision of the outputs implying an accuracy and transparency the underlying code does not support.

The core governance failure is a direct analogue of COMPAS applied to forensic evidence: defendants and their experts have been prevented from examining the source code of the software producing statistical evidence used to convict them. Vendors assert trade secret protection. Courts have split — some requiring disclosure; others accepting vendor validation documentation as sufficient. In multiple cases, software bugs or undisclosed algorithmic changes have been identified only after conviction. Wrongful conviction cases attributable to probabilistic genotyping errors are documented. No federal standard for source code disclosure in probabilistic genotyping cases exists.
tapeItems: COURTS SPLIT ON CONSTITUTIONAL RIGHT TO EXAMINE

harmStats.label:

to examine forensic AI code

Convictions reviewed after
bugs found post-verdict

Vendors who fully disclosed

timeline.event: TrueAllele and STRmix deployed in criminal prosecutions
timeline.detail: Probabilistic genotyping software enters US criminal prosecutions. Vendor-conducted validation studies are completed. Source code is not disclosed. Courts admit likelihood ratio outputs based on vendor documentation of validation methodology.

timeline.event: Defence requests for source code denied — courts split
timeline.detail: Defence attorneys in multiple jurisdictions request source code access. Vendors refuse, citing trade secret. Courts issue conflicting rulings. Defendants in cases where access is denied are convicted on the basis of statistical evidence they cannot independently verify.

timeline.event: STRmix bug disclosed — convictions under review
timeline.detail: A bug in STRmix affecting likelihood ratio calculations under certain conditions is disclosed by the developer. Cases using the affected version are reviewed. The bug was found internally — not through independent validation or adversarial review — and disclosed voluntarily.

timeline.event: Ongoing federal and state litigation — no standard established
timeline.detail: Litigation over source code access continues in federal and state courts. The FBI's use of STRmix in federal prosecutions faces ongoing challenges. Some jurisdictions require limited disclosure under protective orders. No federal standard for source code disclosure in probabilistic genotyping cases exists.

conditions.name: Named Accountable Owner
text: No named official at any prosecution agency or laboratory is documented as accountable for the accuracy of the specific software version used in a specific prosecution before its output is presented as evidence. The vendor provides validation documentation; the laboratory technician applies the software; the prosecutor presents the output — but no named individual has accountability for whether the specific version used in this specific case produced an accurate likelihood ratio for this specific mixture profile.

Software version management is the C1 failure made technical: if no named individual is accountable for confirming which version was used, whether that version had known bugs, and whether those bugs affected the specific calculation, the accuracy question has no owner at the moment it matters — in the individual case.
conditions.quote: The version that convicted a defendant might not be the version that was validated. No named individual was accountable for confirming they were the same.
conditions.source: HSRL C1 analysis — DNA Probabilistic Genotyping

conditions.name: Independent Validation
text: Probabilistic genotyping software has been subject to vendor-conducted and some independent laboratory validation of outputs using known samples. What it has not been subject to is independent source code review — the actual computational steps producing the likelihood ratio. Output validation is necessary but not sufficient: it does not detect bugs or algorithmic choices affecting outputs in cases outside the validation sample's parameter range.

The STRmix bug is the empirical demonstration: not detected by validation studies on known samples; detected internally when the development team reviewed the code. Independent source code review would have identified it before convictions occurred.

conditions.name: Structured Monitoring
text: No structured monitoring tracks probabilistic genotyping performance across all cases — specifically the rate at which likelihood ratios are challenged and successfully rebutted by independent experts. The data exists in case records across thousands of prosecutions. No body aggregates it. The STRmix bug was found internally, not through any external monitoring of case outcomes.

conditions.name: Pre-Established Accountability Protocol
text: No pre-established protocol defines the corroboration required before a probabilistic genotyping output can be used as the primary evidence in a prosecution, or what happens when a defendant's expert identifies a potential error and requests source code access. Courts decide this question case by case with inconsistent results — the C4 absence made systemic.

conditions.name: No Active Suppression
text: Confirmed suppression is the active vendor refusal to disclose source code to defendants in criminal cases — maintained through court proceedings and upheld by some courts as legitimate trade secret protection. Vendors have refused disclosure, litigated the refusal, and prevailed in some courts. The suppression mechanism is legally contested but operationally confirmed.

This is the COMPAS problem applied to forensic evidence: the same trade secret mechanism, the same constitutional question, the same split in court rulings. The prosecution presents a likelihood ratio generated by code the defence cannot examine; the defence must challenge a statistical output without access to the process that produced it.

tags: L-09 Compliance artifacts substitute for safety
L-11 Unnamed experts invoked as authority
laundering.desc: L-11: Likelihood ratios presented with statistical precision implying objective accuracy without disclosing the algorithmic assumptions and code that produced them — unnamed computational authority as evidence. L-09: Vendor validation studies cited as forensic standards compliance, substituting for independent source code review. L-06: Software treated as objective tool; errors attributed to sample quality rather than algorithmic design.

prevention.title: Named laboratory official accountable for version verification in each case
prevention.desc: Before any probabilistic genotyping output is presented as evidence, one named official should document: which version was used, whether that version had known bugs, and whether those bugs could have affected the calculation for this specific mixture. That documentation should be discoverable by the defence as a matter of course.

prevention.title: Mandatory source code escrow with independent expert access for criminal cases
prevention.desc: Any probabilistic genotyping software used in criminal prosecutions should deposit source code in a neutral escrow facility, with access available to cleared independent experts appointed by courts at defence request. Trade secret interests can be protected by court order limiting use to the specific case. The defendant's constitutional right to examine evidence requires it.

prevention.title: National registry of probabilistic genotyping challenges and outcomes
prevention.desc: Every criminal case in which probabilistic genotyping evidence is used and subsequently challenged by a defence expert should be reported to a national forensic science registry. Pattern analysis across cases creates the monitoring signal individual litigation cannot: a pattern of successful challenges to a specific software version signalling a systemic accuracy problem before additional convictions occur.

prevention.title: Federal evidence standard requiring source code disclosure before admission
prevention.desc: Federal rules of evidence — and by extension state equivalents — should require that source code used to produce any algorithmic forensic analysis be made available to the defence before trial as a condition of admissibility. This resolves the current case-by-case litigation by establishing a uniform pre-admission standard.

prevention.title: Statutory prohibition on trade secret claims as a basis for withholding forensic evidence
prevention.desc: No vendor whose software is used in a criminal prosecution may assert trade secret as a basis for withholding source code from the defendant in that prosecution. The commercial interest of a private company cannot override the constitutional rights of a criminal defendant. That principle should be statute, not a question courts decide inconsistently case by case.

comparables.name: COMPAS Risk Assessment
comparables.desc: Exact structural comparator: proprietary algorithm used in criminal proceedings, trade secret protection prevents defendant examination, courts split on constitutional right to examine. COMPAS in sentencing prediction, TrueAllele/STRmix in forensic evidence: same governance failure at different pipeline points.

comparables.name: UK Post Office Horizon
comparables.desc: Cross-domain comparator: software outputs used as primary criminal evidence without independent validation, prosecuting institution resisted accuracy disclosure. Horizon's accounting records and DNA genotyping likelihood ratios are the two clearest cases in the dataset of unvalidated software forming the evidentiary basis for criminal convictions.

comparables.name: Baltimore Stingray Program
comparables.desc: Domain comparator: evidence methodology defendants are prevented from examining — NDA and parallel construction in Stingray, trade secret in genotyping. Both mechanisms remove the adversarial scrutiny criminal justice depends on to identify and correct errors.

metadata.Suppression Confirmed: Yes — active vendor refusal of source code disclosure in criminal proceedings
metadata.Harm Scale: Multiple wrongful convictions documented · courts split on examination rights · no federal standard

palantir-gotham-new-orleans-crime-predic.overviewTitle: A secret surveillance programme the city council didn't know existed for 6 years.
palantir-gotham-new-orleans-crime-predic.overviewNote: Palantir ran a predictive policing and surveillance programme for NOPD from 2012 to 2018. The city council — which had oversight authority — was not informed. The programme was structured as a charitable contribution to avoid contract approval. A journalist discovered it in 2018.
overview: Beginning in 2012, Palantir Technologies provided the New Orleans Police Department with its Gotham data analytics platform — integrating surveillance data, social media monitoring, criminal records, and predictive analytics to identify individuals for proactive investigation. The programme was not disclosed to the New Orleans City Council, which had oversight authority over NOPD contracts. Instead it was structured as a charitable contribution through the New Orleans Business Alliance, enabling it to avoid the contract approval process. NOPD officers were allegedly instructed not to discuss the programme publicly.

The programme's existence was revealed by a 2018 investigation by The Verge, based on public records requests. The investigation documented that Palantir had provided the system without charge initially as a way of building a reference customer, while NOPD used it for surveillance and predictive targeting. When the programme was revealed, the city council voted to terminate it. No individual was held accountable for six years of secret surveillance. This case is the definitive example of a technology vendor and a law enforcement agency using structural mechanisms — charitable giving, corporate structure — to actively avoid the governance oversight that would otherwise apply.
tapeItems: SOCIAL MEDIA + CRIMINAL RECORD + SURVEILLANCE INTEGRATION
NOPD OFFICERS TOLD NOT TO DISCUSS

timeline.event: Palantir begins providing Gotham to NOPD — charitable structure conceals arrangement
timeline.detail: Palantir provides its Gotham data analytics platform to NOPD, structured as a charitable contribution through the New Orleans Business Alliance to avoid city council contract approval. NOPD begins integrating criminal records, social media data, and surveillance feeds. City council not informed.

timeline.event: Secret surveillance — social media monitoring, predictive assessments, no oversight
timeline.detail: NOPD uses Palantir to monitor social media, generate predictive assessments, and build integrated surveillance profiles. Officers allegedly instructed not to discuss the programme publicly. No independent validation of accuracy or equity conducted. City council has no knowledge.

timeline.event: The Verge investigation — programme revealed through public records
timeline.detail: The Verge publishes an investigation documenting the secret programme based on public records. Reveals the charitable giving structure, six years of operation without council knowledge, and scope of surveillance. Creates immediate political crisis in New Orleans.

timeline.event: City council votes to terminate
timeline.detail: New Orleans City Council votes to terminate the Palantir programme. An independent review is commissioned. No individual in NOPD or city administration is held accountable. Palantir denies violation of any law.

conditions.name: Named Accountable Owner
text: No named NOPD official was documented as accountable for the legal basis, accuracy, or equity of the programme. It was structured specifically to avoid the accountability mechanism that would normally apply — city council contract approval — through the charitable giving arrangement. Avoiding the process was avoiding the accountability question.

Officers' alleged instruction not to discuss the programme publicly runs the accountability chain to the person who gave the instruction — who is specifically not publicly named as accountable for the programme's legality or outcomes.
conditions.quote: The programme was structured to avoid the approval process. Avoiding the process is avoiding the accountability question.
conditions.source: HSRL C1 analysis — Palantir New Orleans

conditions.name: Independent Validation
text: The Palantir Gotham programme was never independently validated for accuracy of crime predictions or equity of surveillance targeting. The system integrated multiple data sources without any independent assessment of whether the integrated analysis produced accurate outputs or disproportionately targeted particular communities.

Six-year secret operation is itself a C2 absence consequence: validation requires disclosure. A programme whose existence cannot be disclosed cannot be validated by any body independent of its operators.

conditions.name: Structured Monitoring
text: No structured monitoring was possible given the programme's secrecy. The city council — the body with oversight authority — did not know it existed. The only monitoring was internal to NOPD, by the same officers instructed not to discuss it publicly. The journalist who revealed the programme obtained more information through public records requests than the city council had through six years of ostensible NOPD oversight.

conditions.name: Pre-Established Accountability Protocol
text: No pre-established protocol defined how NOPD would use Palantir's predictive assessments, what evidence threshold triggered police action, what rights individuals had when identified as targets, or how results would be documented. The programme operated without any accountability framework because its existence was designed to remain secret.

conditions.name: No Active Suppression
conditions.finding: PARTIAL — Institutional concealment from council confirmed; individual-level disclosure inconsistent
text: The suppression is institutional: the programme was deliberately structured to avoid council oversight, officers were allegedly instructed not to discuss it, and it operated for six years without disclosure to the body with formal oversight authority. Confirmed institutional suppression at the C5 level.

Scored C5 PARTIAL rather than ABSENT because the suppression operated primarily at the institutional governance level — concealment from the council — rather than including fully confirmed non-disclosure to all individuals subject to surveillance. Institutional suppression is confirmed; individual-level non-disclosure is probable but not fully documented in the available record.

laundering.desc: L-09: The charitable giving structure was a compliance mechanism — technically not a contract — used to avoid the oversight that city contracting would have required. Creates a paper trail suggesting legitimate operation while avoiding the accountability question. L-05: Accountability split between Palantir (the technology) and NOPD (the operation), neither claiming full responsibility.

prevention.title: City council notification required for all police surveillance programmes regardless of funding structure
prevention.desc: The charitable giving structure succeeded because the notification requirement applied to contracts, not to all police operational programmes. An ordinance requiring NOPD to notify the city council of any surveillance programme — regardless of funding mechanism — would have required disclosure before the second year of operation.

prevention.title: Independent equity audit before any predictive analytics programme goes live
prevention.desc: Before NOPD used the Palantir system, an independent equity audit should have assessed whether integrated data sources would produce disproportionately targeted predictions. That audit requires disclosure of the programme — which the secret structure prevented.

prevention.title: Mandatory annual report to city council on all active surveillance programmes
prevention.desc: NOPD should have been required to submit an annual report listing all active surveillance programmes, their scope, funding sources, and number of individuals subject to analysis. This requirement would have compelled disclosure within the first year.

prevention.title: City ordinance requiring council approval for any predictive policing tool
prevention.desc: New Orleans subsequently passed a surveillance technology ordinance requiring council approval. That ordinance should have existed before the programme began. A pre-existing approval requirement would have made the charitable giving workaround legally insufficient.

prevention.title: Protected whistleblower mechanism for officers instructed not to disclose
prevention.desc: If officers were instructed not to discuss the programme publicly, those instructions suppressed disclosure. A legally protected whistleblower mechanism specifically covering officers who disclose secret surveillance programmes to oversight bodies would have created a disclosure pathway independent of investigative journalism.

comparables.name: Chicago Strategic Subject List
comparables.desc: Closest structural comparator: predictive policing without adequate oversight, terminated after external scrutiny. Chicago SSL was disclosed to the council but not to subjects; New Orleans Palantir was concealed from the council. Together they define the two transparency failure modes in US predictive policing.

comparables.name: PRISM / NSA Mass Surveillance
comparables.desc: Scale comparator: secret surveillance whose existence was revealed by external disclosure rather than institutional transparency. PRISM at national scale using classification; New Orleans Palantir at city scale using charitable giving. Same structural failure, different institutional level.

comparables.name: PredPol / Geolitica
comparables.desc: Direct comparator: predictive policing deployed without independent validation. PredPol operated openly but without governance; New Orleans Palantir operated secretly. Together they represent the two governance failure modes for predictive policing — visible but unaccountable, and invisible.

axon-draft-one-ai-police-reports.overviewTitle: AI-written police reports entered into criminal records. No validation. No disclosure to defendants.
axon-draft-one-ai-police-reports.overviewNote: Axon's Draft One system uses AI to generate police incident reports from body camera audio. These AI-drafted reports enter criminal records and are used in prosecutions. No independent accuracy validation has been conducted. Defendants are not told an AI wrote the report used to charge them. Deployed to hundreds of US agencies starting 2023.
overview: Axon Enterprise, the dominant body camera and evidence management company in US law enforcement, launched Draft One in 2023 — an AI system generating draft police incident reports from body camera audio. Officers review and approve the AI-generated draft before submission. The reports enter official criminal records and are used in criminal prosecutions. Axon deployed Draft One to hundreds of US law enforcement agencies with active adoption growing through 2024.

Draft One operates without independent validation of its accuracy across different accents, audio quality conditions, or incident types. No mechanism exists for defendants to know that an AI system generated the report forming the basis for their charge or prosecution, let alone to challenge the AI's characterisation of events. The system's training data, error rates, and failure modes are not publicly disclosed. No federal or state standard governs the use of AI-generated police reports in criminal proceedings. Draft One is the fastest-growing AI deployment in US law enforcement and the case most likely to become the defining governance failure of the next five years if deployment continues without a governance framework.
tapeItems: DEFENDANTS NOT TOLD AI WROTE THE REPORT
FASTEST-GROWING AI IN US LAW ENFORCEMENT

timeline.event: Axon launches Draft One — AI report generation from body camera audio
timeline.detail: Axon launches Draft One, processing body camera audio through a large language model to generate draft police incident reports. Officers review and approve drafts before official submission. No independent accuracy validation published before deployment. No governance framework governs AI-generated content entering criminal records.

timeline.event: Rapid deployment — hundreds of agencies, no governance framework
timeline.detail: Draft One adopted by hundreds of US agencies. AI-generated reports enter criminal records systems and form the evidentiary basis for charges and prosecutions. Defendants not disclosed that AI generated the report. No standard requires disclosure, accuracy validation, or defendant challenge rights.

timeline.event: Accuracy concerns documented — accent and audio quality variation unreported
timeline.detail: Investigative reporting and researcher analysis raise concerns about Draft One accuracy across different accents, audio quality conditions, and incident types. Axon has not published accuracy data disaggregated by these factors. Performance in high-stakes, high-noise, or multilingual scenarios has not been independently assessed.

timeline.event: Legislative attention begins — no federal standard yet
timeline.detail: Several state legislatures consider AI transparency requirements in law enforcement reports. No federal standard established. Axon continues active deployment. Governance is being considered only after hundreds of agencies have adopted the system and AI-generated reports have entered thousands of criminal records.

conditions.name: Named Accountable Owner
text: No named Axon or agency official is documented as accountable for the accuracy of AI-generated reports for a specific agency's population, audio conditions, and incident types before deployment. Each agency's adoption decision was made without a named official certifying that Draft One met an accuracy standard for their jurisdiction's specific conditions.

The officer review step creates an appearance of human accountability that does not constitute genuine accuracy ownership. A fatigued officer approving a plausible-sounding AI draft after a long shift is not exercising the independent judgment that named accountability requires. The approval step is a liability transfer mechanism, not an accuracy assurance mechanism.
conditions.quote: The officer clicks approve. That is not named accountability for the AI's characterisation of what happened.
conditions.source: HSRL C1 analysis — Axon Draft One

conditions.name: Independent Validation
text: Draft One has not been independently validated for accuracy across the range of conditions it encounters in deployment: different accents, audio quality levels, ambient noise, incident types, and officer speaking patterns. The system is a large language model — a class known to produce plausible-sounding but factually incorrect outputs — applied to a domain where inaccurate outputs directly affect criminal charges.

The accuracy validation requirement for AI in criminal reporting is more stringent than most domains: a single inaccurate AI-generated sentence can be the difference between a charge and a non-charge, between conviction and acquittal. That validation has not been conducted or published.

conditions.name: Structured Monitoring
text: No structured monitoring tracks Draft One accuracy across agencies — specifically, the rate at which officer-approved AI drafts are subsequently found to have inaccurately characterised incidents when body camera footage is reviewed by defence attorneys. The body camera footage that Draft One processes is the ground truth against which its accuracy can be assessed. No mechanism requires that comparison to be made systematically.

As Draft One scales to hundreds of agencies, the absence of monitoring creates a growing population of criminal cases in which AI-generated content forms the evidentiary foundation with no systematic accuracy assessment.

conditions.name: Pre-Established Accountability Protocol
text: No pre-established protocol requires disclosure to defendants when an AI system generated the report used to charge them. No protocol defines the accuracy standard an AI-generated report must meet before use in criminal proceedings. No protocol gives defendants a right to compare the AI report against the body camera footage that generated it.

These are not aspirational governance requirements — they are the minimum due process requirements for AI-generated evidence in criminal proceedings. Their absence across every jurisdiction that has adopted Draft One means hundreds of agencies are using AI-generated evidence with no defined accountability framework.

conditions.name: No Active Suppression
text: Confirmed suppression is the non-disclosure to defendants that an AI system generated the report used to charge them. A report generated by an AI language model — with known tendencies to hallucinate plausible but inaccurate content — is materially different from a report written by the officer who witnessed the events. That difference is not disclosed.

Axon's commercial interest in rapid adoption is served by the absence of disclosure requirements: mandatory disclosure would trigger accuracy scrutiny, which would require independent validation, which does not exist. The suppression of AI generation in criminal reports is the predictable consequence of deployment that preceded any governance framework.

tags: L-09 Compliance artifacts substitute for safety
laundering.desc: L-07: Draft One marketed as reducing officer burden and improving consistency — framing AI report generation as an efficiency improvement rather than an evidentiary governance question. L-06: The officer approval step transfers accountability for AI errors to individual officers rather than retaining it at Axon. L-09: Axon's internal quality process and vendor agreements cited as governance, substituting for independent validation.

prevention.title: Named agency official accountable for AI report accuracy before any criminal use
prevention.desc: Before Draft One reports enter criminal records, one named official at each adopting agency should certify the system meets an accuracy standard for their jurisdiction's conditions. Annual review with that person's name on it creates the accountability anchor. The officer approval step is insufficient — it transfers liability without creating accuracy accountability.

prevention.title: Mandatory independent accuracy validation before deployment in criminal proceedings
prevention.desc: Draft One should undergo independent accuracy validation — by a body independent of Axon — across a representative sample of audio conditions, accents, incident types, and officer speaking patterns present in each adopting jurisdiction before its outputs can be used in criminal proceedings.

prevention.title: Mandatory comparison of AI reports against body camera footage in random case samples
prevention.desc: Adopting agencies should be required to compare a random sample of Draft One reports against the body camera footage that generated them, conducted by an independent reviewer quarterly. Discrepancies reported to a state oversight body. This creates the monitoring baseline that currently does not exist across hundreds of agencies.

prevention.title: Mandatory disclosure when AI-generated content forms the basis for a charge
prevention.desc: Any defendant charged on the basis of an AI-generated report should be entitled to: disclosure that AI was used, the specific system and version, the accuracy validation data for that system, and a right to compare the AI report against the underlying body camera footage. These are Brady material obligations — already legally required. The question is whether courts will recognise AI-generated reports as triggering them.

prevention.title: Federal rule requiring disclosure of AI use in all criminal evidence
prevention.desc: Federal Rules of Criminal Procedure should require disclosure of AI involvement in generating any evidence or report used in federal criminal proceedings. The obligation should be automatic — not triggered only by defence request — and should include the AI system's published accuracy data as a required attachment.

comparables.name: COMPAS Risk Assessment
comparables.desc: Structural comparator: proprietary AI in criminal justice without independent validation, defendants cannot challenge the methodology. COMPAS in sentencing prediction, Draft One in evidence generation: same governance failure at different pipeline points.

comparables.name: UK Post Office Horizon
comparables.desc: Cross-domain comparator: software outputs used as primary evidentiary basis in criminal prosecutions without independent validation. Horizon produced accounting records; Draft One produces incident reports. Both cases involve criminal prosecutions built on unvalidated software outputs.

comparables.name: DNA Probabilistic Genotyping
comparables.desc: Closest current domain comparator: proprietary forensic software in criminal prosecutions, defendants seek examination rights, courts split on disclosure. DNA genotyping is the established precedent; Draft One is the emerging version of the same problem at far greater scale.

metadata.Suppression Confirmed: Yes — AI generation not disclosed to defendants
metadata.Harm Scale: Hundreds of agencies · AI reports in criminal records · no federal standard

icedhs-deportation-recommendation-algori.overviewTitle: An algorithm recommended deportation. Agents weren't told how it worked. Neither were the people it targeted.
icedhs-deportation-recommendation-algori.overviewNote: ICE deployed an automated risk-scoring system to recommend detention and deportation priorities. ACLU FOIA analysis documented that the algorithm used proxies correlated with race and national origin. Individuals subject to deportation recommendations had no way to know an algorithm had scored them or to challenge the score. Errors are irreversible — deportation cannot be undone.
overview: US Immigration and Customs Enforcement developed and deployed an Integrated Decision Support (IDAS) tool — an algorithmic risk-scoring system assigning scores to individuals to recommend detention and deportation priorities. The system was deployed beginning around 2018 and used factors producing risk scores that ICE officers used to inform enforcement decisions. The ACLU obtained internal documents through FOIA requests documenting the system's design and use.

ACLU analysis of the IDAS documentation found that the algorithm used factors correlated with race and national origin — including country of birth and prior immigration history — as inputs to risk scoring. Individuals subject to IDAS-informed enforcement had no way to know they had been scored, what their score was, or what factors generated it. ICE officers were not provided with adequate documentation of the algorithm's methodology to assess its accuracy or challenge its outputs. No independent validation of the IDAS algorithm's accuracy or equity was conducted before deployment. The deployment context — federal immigration enforcement — means that errors can result in deportation of individuals with legal presence, family separation, and irreversible harm.

timeline.event: IDAS algorithm deployed — deportation risk scoring begins
timeline.detail: ICE deploys the Integrated Decision Support tool. The algorithm processes immigration records, criminal history, and other factors to produce risk scores. ICE officers use scores to inform enforcement priorities. No independent validation of accuracy or equity before deployment. No named ICE official documented as accountable for algorithm output accuracy.

timeline.event: Algorithm-informed deportation recommendations — individuals and officers not informed of methodology
timeline.detail: ICE officers use IDAS scores to inform detention and deportation decisions. Individuals subject to enforcement are not told they have been scored. Officers are not provided adequate documentation of the methodology to assess whether a score is accurate for a specific individual's circumstances. Algorithm outputs can result in permanent deportation.

timeline.event: ACLU FOIA disclosure — algorithm design and racial proxies documented
timeline.detail: The ACLU obtains internal ICE documents through FOIA requests documenting the IDAS algorithm's design. ACLU analysis finds the algorithm uses factors correlated with race and national origin — including country of birth — as inputs to risk scoring. ACLU publishes findings, triggering congressional attention.

timeline.event: Congressional scrutiny — no independent validation or governance reform yet
timeline.detail: Members of Congress question DHS about IDAS in oversight hearings. DHS defends the algorithm as supporting, not replacing, officer judgment. No independent validation of accuracy or equity published. No federal standard for algorithmic risk scoring in immigration enforcement established.

conditions.name: Named Accountable Owner
text: No named ICE official was documented as accountable for the accuracy or equity of the IDAS algorithm before it was used to inform deportation recommendations. The algorithm was developed and deployed within the federal bureaucratic structure without a named official whose documented role required certifying the algorithm's risk scoring was accurate and lawful before it informed decisions affecting individual liberty.

C1 absence in deportation decisions is irreversibility made structural: if the algorithm's output contributes to a wrongful deportation, there is no named individual whose accountability chain required them to have prevented it.
conditions.quote: The algorithm informed who gets deported. No named individual was accountable for whether it was right.

conditions.name: Independent Validation
text: The IDAS algorithm was not independently validated for accuracy or equity before deployment. The ACLU's finding that the algorithm used proxies correlated with race and national origin — country of birth, immigration history — creates a specific validation requirement: testing outputs against a population in which proxy factors are held constant while the underlying risk factor varies, to assess whether proxies add discriminatory variance beyond their legitimate predictive content. That test was not conducted.

conditions.name: Structured Monitoring
text: No structured monitoring tracked the accuracy of IDAS recommendations — specifically the rate at which high-scoring individuals, when independently reviewed, were found to have been incorrectly scored. The data to conduct this monitoring exists in ICE case records. No mechanism required it to be produced and reviewed by an independent body.

Irreversibility makes C3 especially critical here: in most domains, monitoring can detect errors and enable correction before harm becomes permanent. In deportation, the harm may be completed before any monitoring identifies the error.

conditions.name: Pre-Established Accountability Protocol
text: No pre-established protocol gave individuals subject to IDAS-informed enforcement a right to know their score, the factors generating it, or a mechanism to challenge it before enforcement action was taken. No protocol required ICE officers to document when they relied on a score versus overrode it, creating an accountability gap between algorithm output and enforcement action.

C4 absence in immigration enforcement is structurally distinct from its absence in benefits administration: benefits determinations can be appealed and corrected; deportation may be irreversible by the time any review occurs.

conditions.name: No Active Suppression
text: Suppression confirmed at two levels. First, individuals subject to enforcement were not told they had been scored by an algorithm. Second, the algorithm's existence and methodology were not disclosed until ACLU FOIA litigation — ICE did not proactively disclose to Congress or the public.

A federal agency operating an algorithm scoring individuals for deportation priority without proactively disclosing the system to oversight bodies is exercising a discretionary choice not to disclose — suppression, not omission. The FOIA litigation is the suppression confirmation mechanism.

laundering.desc: L-07: Algorithmic enforcement prioritisation framed as data-driven objective resource allocation rather than discriminatory targeting. L-13: Immigration enforcement mission framing used to deflect scrutiny of racial proxy inputs. L-16: Individuals targeted by the algorithm bear the burden of challenging deportation proceedings without knowing an algorithm was involved.

prevention.title: Named DHS official accountable for IDAS accuracy and equity before deployment
prevention.desc: Before the IDAS algorithm informed a single deportation recommendation, one named DHS official should have certified — with name on a pre-deployment record — that the algorithm's risk scoring was accurate and did not use discriminatory proxies. Named accountability with the algorithm's documented inputs and validation results attached creates the anchor that is currently absent.

prevention.title: Independent equity validation of all algorithmic inputs before deployment
prevention.desc: Every IDAS input — including country of birth and immigration history — should have been independently tested to assess whether it functions as a legitimate risk predictor or a discriminatory proxy for race and national origin. Testing by a body independent of ICE, with results published before any enforcement use.

prevention.title: Mandatory tracking of IDAS-informed enforcement outcomes by demographic group
prevention.desc: For every enforcement action informed by an IDAS score: the score, the decision taken, and the outcome of any subsequent legal review — aggregated quarterly and reported to DHS OIG and congressional oversight committees. This is the monitoring baseline that enables detection of systematic bias in operational outputs.

prevention.title: Right of individuals to know they have been scored before enforcement action
prevention.desc: Any individual subject to enforcement action informed by an IDAS score should be entitled to know: that they have been scored, what their score is, what factors generated it, and a mechanism to challenge the score before the enforcement action is completed. In proceedings where deportation is possible, this is a due process minimum.

prevention.title: Mandatory proactive disclosure of all algorithmic tools used in immigration enforcement
prevention.desc: ICE and DHS should be required to proactively disclose to Congress and the public: all algorithmic tools used in immigration enforcement decisions, their design documentation, input factors, and accuracy data. Proactive disclosure — not requiring FOIA litigation — is the minimum transparency standard for federal agencies using algorithms affecting individual liberty.

comparables.name: COMPAS Risk Assessment
comparables.desc: Closest structural comparator: algorithmic risk scoring in a legal proceeding affecting individual liberty, racial proxy inputs, no independent validation, subjects cannot challenge the score. COMPAS in criminal sentencing, IDAS in deportation: the same governance failure applied to the two most consequential legal proceedings individuals face in the US system.

comparables.name: Netherlands Toeslagenaffaire
comparables.desc: Cross-domain comparator: government algorithm using nationality and origin as a risk factor, subjects not informed, confirmed institutional suppression. Toeslagenaffaire used dual nationality in fraud scoring; ICE IDAS used country of birth in deportation risk scoring. Same discriminatory proxy mechanism, different domain.

comparables.name: Clearview AI
comparables.desc: Direct operational companion case: Clearview AI was used by ICE in immigration enforcement alongside predictive risk scoring. Together, IDAS and Clearview define the two AI governance failures in federal immigration enforcement — risk scoring for targeting, facial recognition for identification.
